devotional.title: Foundations and Structure
devotional.content:
{AtlasWarmJ}{esus}
concluded His Sermon on the Mount with a parable about two builders. One 
built his house on rock; when the storms came, it stood firm. The other 
built on sand; the rains fell, the floods came, and great was its fall.

In mathematics, the real number system is our rock. Every equation we 
solve, every function we graph, every proof we construct rests on the 
properties you have learned in this chapter. If these foundations are 
shaky, everything built upon them will wobble. But if they are solid, 
you can build structures of remarkable beauty and power.

The order of operations, the properties of arithmetic, the structure of 
the real numbers—these are not arbitrary rules invented by 
mathematicians to torment students. They are discoveries about the 
deep structure of reality, as fixed and reliable as the laws of physics. 
When you master them, you are not learning tricks; you are learning to 
think with the same logic that underlies creation itself.

As you move forward in your study of algebra, return to this chapter 
whenever you feel uncertain. These foundations will support everything 
that follows.
devotional.reflection:
Mathematics is often called the "queen of the 
sciences" because its truths are absolutely certain—$2 + 2$ will 
always equal $4$. What does this certainty suggest about the nature of 
truth itself? How might mathematical truth point to its divine source?

epigraph.text:
Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart, 
as working for the Lord, not for human masters.
epigraph.quote: Do not worry about your difficulties in mathematics. I can assure you mine are still greater.

content.text: {Do not worry about your difficulties in mathematics. I can assure you mine are still greater.}{Albert Einstein}

This chapter has laid the foundation for your study of algebra. You have 
learned to classify real numbers, perform operations with signed numbers, 
understand absolute value, and apply the order of operations. These skills 
will be used throughout the course and beyond. Take time to review the 
key concepts before moving on.
Match each term with its definition or example.

items: Natural numbers A. Numbers like $\sqrt{2}$ and $\pi$
Absolute value E. $a(b + c) = ab + ac$
Opposite F. Numbers that can be written as $\frac{a}{b}$
Additive identity G. Distance from zero
Distributive property H. For $a$, the number $-a$

content.text:
Classify each number by all sets to which it belongs (natural, whole, 
integer, rational, irrational, real):
Write each fraction as a decimal. Identify as terminating or repeating.
Write each repeating decimal as a fraction in lowest terms.
True or false? Justify your answer.

items: Every natural number is a rational number.
Zero is neither positive nor negative.
The sum of two irrational numbers is always irrational.

content.text: Find the missing value and state the property.
Find the distance between each pair of numbers.
For what values of $x$ is $\abs{x - 3} = x - 3$?

A scuba diver descends from sea level to $-45$ feet, rises $20$ feet, 
then descends another $30$ feet. Express the final depth and the total 
distance traveled.
